race.name: La Transjurassienne
race.tagline: France's toughest ski marathon — 68 hilly kilometers through the Jura Mountains where snow is never guaranteed but suffering always is.

vitals.date: Second or third Sunday in February
vitals.aid_stations: Multiple stations with French specialties and warm drinks

climate.description: February in the Jura Mountains delivers variable conditions. The moderate altitude (800-1,200m) makes snow reliability the race's biggest challenge — multiple cancellations in recent decades due to insufficient snow. When conditions are good, expect -8°C to 0°C with forest shelter.
challenges: Snow reliability is the race's Achilles heel — multiple cancellations
Moderate altitude means rain is possible even in February
Hilly terrain with 1,000m+ of climbing tests endurance deeply
Variable conditions within the same race day as altitude changes

course.primary: Point-to-point through the Jura Mountains from Lamoura to Mouthe, traversing the length of the Jura range. The course is relentlessly hilly with over 1,000m of climbing, passing through dense conifer forest. One of the most physically demanding courses on the Worldloppet circuit.
course.surface: Machine-groomed tracks through mountain forest terrain
features: Over 1,000m of climbing makes it one of the hilliest Worldloppet races
Dense Jura conifer forest creates atmospheric, sheltered skiing
Offers both classic and skate on the full 68km distance
Mouthe finish — known as the coldest village in France
French feed stations with regional cheese, warm wine, and pastries

nordic_lab_rating.score_note: A proper mountain marathon with serious climbing. Strong across the board but snow reliability issues have caused multiple cancellations, preventing a higher overall score. When it runs, it delivers one of the toughest days in citizen skiing.
nordic_lab_rating.tier_note: Prestige 4 promotes from base TIER 2 — stays TIER 2 (cannot enter TIER 1)

history.summary: Founded in 1979, La Transjurassienne traverses the Jura Mountains in eastern France, covering 68km with over 1,000m of climbing. It is France's premier long-distance ski race and a proud member of the Worldloppet series. The race has faced increasing challenges with snow reliability in recent years due to the moderate altitude of the Jura range, leading to several cancellations and course modifications.
notable_facts: France's premier long-distance cross-country ski race since 1979
The finish town of Mouthe holds France's cold temperature record (-36.7°C in 1968)
Has been cancelled or shortened multiple times due to insufficient snow
The Jura Mountains are the birthplace of French competitive nordic skiing
The 1,000m+ of climbing makes it one of the most physically demanding Worldloppet races
